text: On the easy puzzle (puzzle_001, n=3-4) the cost CIs of none/medium/coarse overlap at 1 sigma; only "fine is reproducibly the most expensive" survives. The pilot "coarse ~2x cheaper" headline is invalidated at low difficulty.
report:
## Why these numbers don't support a cost difference

Each toolset (**none / medium / coarse / fine**) was run repeatedly on the easy puzzle (`puzzle_001`, n = 3–4 runs). For each we took the **mean cost-to-solve** and drew a **confidence interval (CI)** = mean ± one standard deviation (**1 sigma**) — an error bar capturing run-to-run noise.

**"Overlap at 1 sigma"** means the error bars of none / medium / coarse all touch: each mean sits inside the others' ±1σ band. When bars overlap like that, the apparent ordering between them is within noise — rerun the experiment and the order could flip. So we **cannot** claim any of none / medium / coarse is cheaper than the others here.

The one separation that survives is **fine**: its band sits clearly above the rest, so *"fine is reproducibly the most expensive"* is a real effect.

**Consequence:** the pilot's headline *"coarse ≈ 2× cheaper"* was an artifact of too few runs at low difficulty — it does not survive once the error bars are drawn. That is why this answer is **refuted**.
